author: John Chrysostom
book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

title: Homily XXVI on Acts xii. 1, 2.

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now at that time Herod the King stretched forth his hands to vex certain of the Church.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he killed James the brother of John with the sword.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And because he saw it pleased the Jews, he proceeded further to take Peter also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then were the days of unleavened bread.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “At that time,” of course meaning the time immediately following: for this is the custom of Scripture.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he well says that Herod “the king” (did this): this was not he of Christ’s time.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo, a different sort of trial—and mark what I said in the beginning, how things are blended, how rest and trouble alternate in the whole texture of the history—not now the Jews, nor the Sanhedrim, but the king.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Greater the power, the warfare more severe, the more it was done to obtain favor with the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And,” it says, “he slew James the brother of John with the sword:” (taking him) at random and without selection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, should any raise a question, why God permitted this, we shall say, that it was for the sake of these (Jews) themselves: thereby, first, convincing them, that even when slain (the Apostles) prevail, just as it was in the case of Stephen: secondly, giving them opportunity, after satiating their rage, to recover from their madness; thirdly, showing them that it was by His permission this was done.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he saw,” it says, “that it pleased the Jews, he proceeded further to seize Peter also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: O excessive wickedness!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On whose behalf was it, that he gratified them by doing murders thus without plan or reason?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And it was the day of unleavened bread.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, the idle preciseness of the Jews: to kill indeed they forbade not, but at such a time they did such things!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Whom having arrested, he put in ward, having delivered him to four quaternions of soldiers.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This was done both of rage, and of fear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He slew,” it says, “James the brother of John with the sword.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark their courage?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, that none may say that without danger or fear of danger they brave death, as being sure of God’s delivering them, therefore he permits some to be put to death, and chief men too, Stephen and James, thereby convincing their slayers themselves, that not even these things make them fall away, and hinder them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Peter therefore was kept in prison: but prayer was made without ceasing of the Church unto God for him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the contest was now for life and death: both the slaying of the one made them fearful, and the casting of the other into prison.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when Herod would have brought him forth, the same night Peter was sleeping between two soldiers, bound with two chains; and the keepers before the door kept the prison.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, behold, the angel of the Lord came upon him, and a light shined in the prison: and he smote Peter on the side, and raised him up, saying, Arise up quickly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And his chains fell off from his hands.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In that night He delivered him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And a light shined in the prison,” that he might not deem it fancy: and none saw the light, but he only.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if, notwithstanding this was done, he thought it a fancy, because of its unexpectedness; if this had not been, much more would he have thought this: so prepared was he for death.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For his having waited there many days and not being saved caused this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then, say you, did He not suffer him to fall into the hands of Herod, and then deliver him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because that would have brought people into astonishment, whereas this was credible: and they would not even have been thought human beings.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But in the case of Stephen, what did He not do?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Did He not show them his face as it had been the face of an angel?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But what in short did He leave undone here also?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the angel said to him, Gird thyself, and bind on thy sandals.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here again it shows, that it was not done of craft: for one that is in haste and wishes to break out (of prison), is not so particular as to take his sandals, and gird himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he did so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he said unto him, Put on thy cloak, and follow me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he went out, and followed him; and wist not that it was true which was done by the Angel; but thought he saw a vision.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When they were past the first and the second ward, they came unto the iron gate that leadeth unto the city; which opened to them of its own accord.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Behold, a second miracle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they went out, and passed on through one street; and forthwith the angel departed from him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when Peter was come to himself, he said, Now I know of a surety, that the Lord hath sent His Angel, and hath delivered me out of the hand of Herod, and from all the expectation of the people of the Jews.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When the angel departed, then Peter understood: “Now I perceive,” says he, not then.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But why is this so, and why is Peter not sensible of the things taking place, although he had already experienced a like deliverance when all were released? (ch. v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (The Lord) would have the pleasure come to him all at once, and that he should first be at liberty, and then be sensible of what had happened.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The circumstance also of the chains having fallen off from his hands, is a strong argument of his not having fled.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he had considered the thing, he came to the house of Mary the mother of John, whose surname was Mark; where many were gathered together praying.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how Peter does not immediately withdraw, but first brings the good tidings to his friends.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And as Peter knocked at the door of the gate, a damsel came to hearken, named Rhoda.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when she knew Peter’s voice, she opened not the gate for gladness,”—Mark even the servant-girls, how full of piety they are,—“but ran in, and told how Peter stood before the gate.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they, though it was so, shook their heads (incredulously): “And they said unto her, Thou art mad.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But she constantly affirmed that it was even so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they said, It is his angel.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But Peter continued knocking: and when they had opened the door, and saw him, they were astonished.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he, beckoning unto them with the hand to hold their peace, declared unto them how the Lord had brought him out of the prison.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he said, Go show these things unto James, and to the brethren.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he departed, and went into another place.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us review the order of the narrative.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “At that time,” it says, “Herod the king stretched forth his hands to afflict certain of the Church.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Like a wild beast, he attacked all indiscriminately and without consideration.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is what Christ said: “My cup indeed ye shall drink, and with the baptism wherewith I am baptized, shall ye be baptized.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 39.) (b) “And he killed James the brother of John.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there was also another James, the brother of the Lord: therefore to distinguish him, he says, “The brother of John.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark that the sum of affairs rested in these three, especially Peter and James? (a) And how was it he did not kill Peter immediately?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It mentions the reason: “it was the day of unleavened bread:” and he wished rather to make a display (ἐκπομπεὕσαι) with the killing of him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he saw it pleased the Jews.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For their own part, they now in consequence of Gamaliel’s advice, abstained from bloodshedding: and besides, did not even invent accusations; but by means of others they compassed the same results. (c) This (counsel of Gamaliel’s) above all was their condemnation: for the preaching was shown to be no longer a thing of men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He proceeded further to kill Peter also.” (ch. v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In very deed was that fulfilled, “We are accounted as sheep for the slaughter.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Seeing,” it says, “it was a pleasing thing to the Jews.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A pleasing thing, bloodshed, and unrighteous bloodshed, wickedness, impiety!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He ministered to their senseless (ἀτόποις) lusts: for, whereas he ought to have done the contrary, to check their rage, he made them more eager, as if he were an executioner, and not a physician to their diseased minds.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (And this) though he had numberless warnings in the case of both his grandfather and his father Herod, how the former in consequence of his putting the children to death suffered the greatest calamities, and the latter by slaying John raised up against himself a grievous war.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But as they thought * * He feared lest Peter, in consequence of the slaying of James, should withdraw; and wishing to have him in safe keeping, he put him in prison: “and delivered him to four quaternions of soldiers” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 4): the stricter the custody, the more wondrous the display.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Peter therefore was kept in prison.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this was all the better for Peter, who was thereby made more approved, and evinced his own manly courage.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it says, “there was earnest prayer making.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was the prayer of (filial) affection: it was for a father they asked, a father mild.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “There was,” it says, “earnest prayer.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hear how they were affected to their teachers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No factions, no perturbation: but they betook them to prayer, to that alliance which is indeed invincible, to this they betook them for refuge.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They did not say, “What?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I, poor insignificant creature that I am, to pray for him!” for, as they acted of love, they did not give these things a thought.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe, it was during the feast, that (their enemies) brought these trials upon them, that their worth might be the more approved.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when Herod,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See Peter sleeping, and not in distress or fear!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That same night, after which he was to be brought forth, he slept, having cast all upon God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Between two soldiers, bound with two chains.” (comp.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark, how strict the ward!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And says, Arise.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The guards were asleep with him, and therefore perceived nothing of what was happening.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And a light shined.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What was the light for?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In order that Peter might see as well as hear, and not imagine it to be all fancy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the command, “Arise quickly,” that he may not be remiss.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He also smote him; so deeply did he sleep. (a) “Rise,” says he, “quickly:” this is not to hurry him (θορυβοὕντος) but to persuade him not to delay. (c) “And” immediately “his chains fell off from his hands.” (b) How? answer me: where are the heretics?—let them answer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the Angel said unto him,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 8) by this also convincing him that it is no fancy: to this end he bids him gird himself and put on his shoes, that he may shake off his sleep, and know that it is real. (a) (e) “And he wist not that it was true that was done by the Angel, but thought he saw a vision” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 9): (e) well he might, by reason of the excessive greatness (ὑπερβολὴν) of the things taking place.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark what a thing it is for a miracle to be excessive (ὑπερβολὴ σημείου)? how it amazes (ἐκπλήττει) the beholder? how it will not let the thing be believed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if Peter “thought he saw a vision,” though he had girded himself and put on his shoes, what would have been the case with another?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And,” it says, “when they had passed the first and the second ward, they came to the iron gate, which opened unto them of its own accord” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10): and yet the things that had happened within (the prison) were more marvellous: but this was now more after the manner of man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And having gone out, they went along one street and immediately (all ‘until’) the Angel departed from him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When there was no hindrance, then the Angel departed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For Peter would not have gone along (προἥλθεν), there being so many hindrances.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he came to himself:” for in very truth, it was indeed an amazement (ἔκπληξις).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now,” saith he, “I know”—now, not then, when I was in the prison,—“that the Lord hath sent His Angel, and hath delivered me out of the hand of Herod and from all the expectation of the people of the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when he had considered” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 12), it says: viz. where he was, or, that he must not without more ado depart but requite his Benefactor: “he came to the house of Mary the mother of John.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who is this John?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Probably he that was always with them: for this is why he adds his distinctive name (τὸ παράσημον), “whose surname was Mark.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe, “praying” in the night, how much they got by it: what a good thing affliction is; how wakeful it made them!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see how great the gain resulting from the death of Stephen? do you see how great the benefit accruing from this imprisonment?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is not by taking vengeance upon those who wronged them that God shows the greatness of the Gospel: but in the wrong-doers themselves, without any harm happening to those, he shows what a mighty thing the afflictions in themselves are, that we may not seek in any wise deliverance from them, nor the avenging of our wrongs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And mark how the very servant-girls were henceforth upon an equality with them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For joy,” it says, “she opened not.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This too is well done, that they likewise may not be amazed by seeing him at once, and that they may be incredulous, and their minds may be exercised.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But ran in,” etc. just as we are wont to do, she was eager to be herself the bringer of the good tidings, for good news it was indeed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they said unto her, Thou art mad: but she constantly affirmed that it was even so: then said they, It is his Angel.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is a truth, that each man has an Angel.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what would the Angel?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was from the time (of night) that they surmised this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when he “continued knocking, and when they had opened, and saw him, they were astonished.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he beckoning to them with his hand” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 16, 17), made them keep quiet, to hear all that had happened to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was now an object of more affectionate desire to the disciples, not only in consequence of his being saved, but by his sudden coming in upon them and straightway departing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now, both his friends learn all clearly; and the aliens also learn, if they had a mind, but they had not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The same thing happened in the case of Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Tell these things,” he says, “to James, and to the brethren.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How free from all vainglory!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nor did he say, Make known these things to people everywhere, but, “to the brethren.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he withdrew to another place:” for he did not tempt God, nor fling himself into temptation: since, when they were commanded to do this, then they did it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Go,” it was said, “speak in the temple to the people.” (ch. v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this the Angel said not (here); on the contrary, by silently removing him and bringing him out by night, he gave him free permission to withdraw—and this too is done, that we may learn that many things are providentially brought about after the manner of men—so that he should not again fall into peril.—For that they may not say, “It was his Angel,” after he was gone, they say this first, and then they see himself overthrowing their notion of the matter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Had it been the Angel, he would have knocked at the door, would not have retired to another place.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what followed in the day, make them sure.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “So Peter was kept in the prison,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They, being at large, were at prayer: he, bound, was in sleep.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he wist not that it was true.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If he thought it was true that was happening, he would have been astonished, he would not have remembered (all the circumstances): but now, seeming to be in a dream, he was free from perturbation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “When,” it says, “they were past the first and the second ward”—see also how strong the guard was—“they came unto the iron gate.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now know I that the Lord hath sent His Angel.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why is not this effected by themselves?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (I answer,) By this also the Lord honors them, that by the ministry of His Angels he rescues them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then why was it not so in the case of Paul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There with good reason, because the jailer was to be converted, whereas here, it was only that the Apostle should be released. (ch. xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And God disposes all things in divers ways.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And there too, it is beautiful, that Paul sings hymns, while here Peter was asleep.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he had considered, he came to the house of Mary,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us not hide God’s marvels, but for our own good let us study to display these abroad for the edifying of the others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as he deserves to be admired for choosing to be put into bonds, so is he worthy of more admiration, that he withdrew not until he had reported all to his friends.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he said, Tell James and the brethren.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That they may rejoice: that they may not be anxious.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Through these those learn, not those through him: such thought had he for the humbler part!—

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Truly, nothing better than affliction not above measure (συμμέτρου).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What think you must have been their state of mind—how full of delight!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Where now are those women, who sleep the whole night through?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Where are those men, who do not even turn themselves in their bed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou the watchful soul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: With women, and children, and maid-servants, they sang hymns to God, made purer than the sky by affliction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But now, if we see a little danger, we fall back.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing ever was more splendid than that Church.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us imitate these, let us emulate them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not for this was the night made, that we should sleep all through it and be idle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To this bear witness the artisans, the carriers, and the merchants (to this), the Church of God rising up in the midst of the night.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Rise thou up also, and behold the quire of the stars, the deep silence, the profound repose: contemplate with awe the order (οἰκονομίαν) of thy Master’s household.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then is thy soul purer: it is lighter, and subtler, and soaring disengaged: the darkness itself, the profound silence, are sufficient to lead thee to compunction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And if also thou look to the heavens studded with its stars, as with ten thousand eyes, if thou bethink thee that all those multitudes who in the daytime are shouting, laughing, frisking, leaping, wronging, grasping, threatening, inflicting wrongs without number, lie all one as dead, thou wilt condemn all the self-willedness of man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Sleep hath invaded and defeated (ἡλεγξεν) nature: it is the image of death, the image of the end of all things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If thou (look out of window and) lean over into the street, thou wilt not hear even a sound: if thou look into the house, thou wilt see all lying as it were in a tomb.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: All this is enough to arouse the soul, and lead it to reflect on the end of all things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here indeed my discourse is for both men and women.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Bend thy knees, send forth groans, beseech thy Master to be merciful: He is more moved by prayers in the night, when thou makest the time for rest a time for mourning.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Remember what words that king uttered: “I have been weary with my groaning: every night will I wash my bed, I will water my couch with my tears.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: However delicate a liver thou mayest be, thou art not more delicate than he: however rich thou mayest be, thou art not richer than David.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again the same Psalmist saith, “At midnight I rose to give thanks unto Thee for the judgments of Thy righteousness.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No vainglory then intrudes upon thee: how can it, when all are sleeping, and not looking at thee?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then neither sloth nor drowsiness invades thee: how can they, when thy soul is aroused by such great things?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: After such vigils come sweet slumbers and wondrous revelations.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do this, thou also the man, not the woman only.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let the house be a Church, consisting of men and women.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For think not because thou art the only man, or because she is the only woman there, that this is any hindrance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For where two,” He saith, “are gathered together in My Name, there am I in the midst of them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Where Christ is in the midst, there is a great multitude.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Where Christ is, there needs must Angels be, needs must Archangels also and the other Powers be there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then ye are not alone, seeing ye have Him Who is Lord of all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hear again the prophet also saying, “Better is one that doeth the will of the Lord, than ten thousand transgressors.” (comp.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ecclus. xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing more weak than a multitude of unrighteous men, nothing more strong than one man who lives according to the law of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If thou hast children wake up them also, and let thy house altogether become a Church through the night: but if they be tender, and cannot endure the watching, let them stay for the first or second prayer, and then send them to rest: only stir up thyself, establish thyself in the habit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing is better than that storehouse which receives such prayers as these.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hear the Prophet speaking: “If I remembered Thee upon my bed, I thought upon Thee in the dawn of the morning.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But you will say: I have labored much during the day, and I cannot.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mere pretext this and subterfuge.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For however much thou hast labored, thou wilt not toil like the smith, who lets fall such a heavy hammer from a great height upon the (metal flying off in) sparks, and takes in the smoke with his whole body: and yet at this work he spends the greater part of the night.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ye know also how the women, if there is need for us to go into the country, or to go forth unto a vigil, watch through the whole night.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then have thou also a spiritual forge, to fashion there not pots or cauldrons, but thine own soul, which is far better than either coppersmith or goldsmith can fashion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thy soul, waxen old in sins, cast thou into the smelting-furnace of confession: let fall the hammer from on high: that is, the condemnation of thy words (τὥν ῥημάτων τὴν κατάγνωσιν): light up the fire of the Spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou hast a far mightier craft (than theirs).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou art beating into shape not vessels of gold, but the soul, which is more precious than all gold, even as the smith hammers out his vessel.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is no material vessel that thou art working at, but thou art freeing thy soul from all imaginations belonging to this life.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let a lamp be by thy side, not that one which we burn, but that which the prophet had, when he said, “Thy law is a lamp unto my feet.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Bring thy soul to a red heat, by prayer: when thou seest it hot enough, draw it out, and mould it into what shape thou wilt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Believe me, not fire so effectual to burn off rust, as night prayer to remove the rust of our sins.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let the night-watchers, if no one else, shame us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They, by man’s law, go their rounds in the cold, shouting loudly, and walking through lanes (στενωπὥν) and alleys, oftentimes drenched with rain and (all) congealed with cold, for thee and for thy safety, and the protection of thy property.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is he taking such care for thy property, while thou takest none even for thy soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet I do not make thee go thy rounds in the open air like him, nor shout loudly and rend thy sides: but in thy closet itself, or in thy bedchamber, bend thy knees, and entreat thy Lord.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why did Christ Himself pass a whole night on the mountain?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Was it not, that He might be an ensample to us?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then is it that the plants respire, in the night, I mean: and then also does the soul take in the dew even more than they.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What the sun has parched by day becomes cool again at night.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: More refreshing than all dew, the tears of the night descend upon our lusts and upon all heat and fever of the soul, and do not let it be affected in any such way.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if it do not enjoy the benefit of that dew, it will be burnt up in the daytime.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But God forbid (it should be so)!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Rather, may we all, being refreshed, and enjoying the mercy of God, be freed from the burden of our sins, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father together with the Holy Spirit be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
